notes: searches: ~5, clusters: 6 (Miller biography via local Bliss; Millerite 457BC->1844 chronology chain [doctrinally central]; 1833 Leonid meteor storm [doctrinally central]; Josiah Litch/Ottoman 1840 [doctrinally central]; postmillennialism history; medieval vernacular Bible access via local Deanesly [doctrinally central]). Bible-text attributed_quotes resolved via KJV common-knowledge fast-path (empty sources). Biography cluster leans on local primary Bliss (Memoirs of William Miller 1853) which White drew from. Control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims: GC-18-049 (no smuggled fact -> unverifiable), GC-18-091 (smuggles verifiable biblical+biographical facts -> supported), GC-18-122 (smuggles biographical fact -> supported). Consistency: 457 vs 458 BC split treated as disputed per ch22 verifier.

GC-18-001.rationale: Bliss's insider biography documents Miller's early poverty and the lessons of self-denial it taught; consistent with the source White used.

sources.title: S. Bliss, Memoirs of William Miller (1853)

GC-18-002.rationale: Bliss records Miller's father as Captain William Miller, an officer of the Revolutionary period (later also a militia/regular-army captain). Standard in the insider biography.

GC-18-052.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate to the narrative of Daniel 8:15-27: Gabriel is told to make Daniel understand yet gives no starting point and Daniel does not fully grasp it.

GC-18-053.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Daniel 8:27, correctly attributed to Daniel.

GC-18-054.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Daniel 9:22-23, correctly attributed to the angel's return to Daniel.

GC-18-055.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Daniel 9:24-27, correctly attributed.

GC-18-056.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Hebrew nechtak/chathak (Dan 9:24) is a hapax legomenon whose root sense is 'to cut, cut off/divide'; standard lexicons (Gesenius, BDB) give this literal derivation. The contextual gloss 'determine/decree' is compatible with a root meaning 'cut off'.

GC-18-057.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The seventy weeks = 490 years (year-day) 'pertaining to thy people' (Dan 9:24) is the traditional messianic-prophetic reading, standard in evangelical scholarship (e.g. Hoehner). Critical scholarship reads the weeks differently, but the 70x7=490 and 'thy people = Jews' equivalence follows the text.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-058.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The premise that Daniel 9's seventy weeks are 'cut off' from Daniel 8's 2300 days, so both share a starting point, is a distinctive Millerite/Adventist interpretive link. Mainstream critical scholarship reads Dan 8:14's 2300 evenings-mornings as concerning Antiochus IV (2nd c. BC) and unconnected to Dan 9; even most evangelical interpreters do not merge the two periods. Genuine interpretive dispute.
GC-18-058.modern_consensus: Mainstream scholarship does not connect the 2300 evenings-mornings of Dan 8 with the seventy weeks of Dan 9.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-059.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate to Daniel 9:25: the seventy weeks date 'from the going forth of the commandment to restore and to build Jerusalem.'

GC-18-060.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The Ezra 7 decree of Artaxerxes I is genuine, but its date is contested: the king's 7th year falls in 458 BC on standard Persian accession-year chronology, or 457 BC on the non-accession/Jewish civil-year reckoning White's sources used. Consistent with the 457-vs-458 split recorded by the ch22 verifier. Some scholars instead take Artaxerxes' later (445/444 BC) decree to Nehemiah as the terminus.
GC-18-060.modern_consensus: Artaxerxes I's 7th year is dated 458 BC (accession reckoning) or 457 BC (non-accession); the terminus a quo of Dan 9 is disputed among 457, 458, and 445 BC.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-061.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Ezra 6:14 (house built 'according to the commandment of Cyrus, and Darius, and Artaxerxes king of Persia').

GC-18-062.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The 'composite decree' argument (that the three kings' successive edicts together constitute the single prophetic decree fixing the start of the 2300 years) is a Millerite/Adventist interpretive device. Mainstream scholarship treats the decrees of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes as distinct royal acts and does not combine them to anchor a 2300-year period.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-063.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. 'Autumn of 457 BC' depends both on the disputed 457-vs-458 dating and on a Jewish civil-calendar (autumn-to-autumn) reckoning particular to the Adventist chronology; the effective date of Ezra's return is not independently fixed to autumn 457 BC in mainstream scholarship.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-064.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The arithmetic is correct: 483 years from 457 BC, with no year zero, reach AD 27. The result is valid given the (disputed, see GC-18-063) starting premise; the calculation itself is sound.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)

GC-18-065.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The date of Jesus' baptism/start of ministry is debated (roughly AD 26-29); 'autumn of AD 27' is one interpretive placement tied to the 457 BC premise, not a fixed historical datum. Luke 3:1 (15th year of Tiberius) is itself variously reckoned.
GC-18-065.modern_consensus: Jesus' baptism/ministry began c. AD 26-29; no consensus fixes it to autumn AD 27.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-066.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Acts 10:38, correctly attributed to Peter.

GC-18-067.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Luke 4:18 (citing Isa 61:1), correctly attributed to Jesus.

GC-18-068.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate to Mark 1:14-15: after his baptism Jesus preached the gospel of the kingdom in Galilee, 'the time is fulfilled.'

GC-18-069.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The framing of AD 27-34 as a fixed span during which the gospel went 'especially to the Jews' is part of the Adventist prophetic scheme; the endpoints depend on the disputed chronology (GC-18-065/073). The historical mission-to-Jews-then-Gentiles pattern is real but not tied to those exact dates.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-070.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Matthew 10:5-6, correctly attributed to Jesus.

GC-18-071.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. AD 31 for the crucifixion is a minority position; modern NT chronology favors AD 30 or AD 33 (constrained by Nisan 14 falling on a Friday), while dates 29-31 were more common in older scholarship. AD 31 flows from the disputed 457 BC premise and lacks strong astronomical support, but the crucifixion date is a genuinely debated question, so 'disputed' rather than 'contradicted.'
GC-18-071.modern_consensus: The two astronomically viable crucifixion years are AD 30 and AD 33; AD 31 has little modern support.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-072.rationale: The 'four thousand years' of the sacrificial system rests on Ussher-type biblical chronology (creation c. 4004 BC, sacrifice from Abel onward), standard in the 19th-century Protestant sources White used. Modern archaeology and history reject a c. 4000 BC creation and any 4,000-year continuous Israelite sacrificial system, but the figure is period-typical rather than idiosyncratic.
GC-18-072.period_historiography: Ussherian biblical chronology (creation c. 4004 BC), universal in the 19th-century sources behind GC, yields roughly 4,000 years from creation/Fall to the crucifixion.
GC-18-072.modern_consensus: No scientific basis for a c. 4000 BC creation; organized Israelite sacrifice dates to the 2nd/1st millennium BC at earliest.

GC-18-073.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Ending the seventy weeks in AD 34 is interpretive: it is anchored to the (undated) martyrdom of Stephen, usually placed c. AD 34-36, and to the disputed 457 BC start. Not an independently fixed date.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-074.rationale: The events (Stephen's martyrdom before the Sanhedrin, and the ensuing persecution of Christ's followers) are recorded in Acts 6-8 (common knowledge). The specific dating to c. AD 34 and its role as 'sealing' Jewish rejection is the interpretive Millerite placement, not independently fixed.

GC-18-075.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate to Acts 8:5 (Philip preached Christ in Samaria).

GC-18-076.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate to Acts 10 (Peter, divinely directed, brings the gospel to Cornelius the centurion at Caesarea).

GC-18-077.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate to Acts (Paul's conversion; Acts 22:21 commission 'far hence unto the Gentiles').

GC-18-078.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The claim that the seventy weeks are 'fixed beyond question' at 457 BC-AD 34 overstates the case; both endpoints rest on the contested chronology (GC-18-060/073). Not beyond question in scholarship.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-079.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The arithmetic (2300 - 490 = 1810) is correct; it is valid given the interpretive premise that the 490 years are a subset of the 2300 (that premise is disputed at GC-18-058).

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)

GC-18-080.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The arithmetic (AD 34 + 1810 = 1844) is correct. The result is valid given the disputed premises upstream.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)

GC-18-081.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. That the 2300 days of Dan 8:14 terminate in 1844 is the central Adventist prophetic conclusion, rejected by mainstream Daniel scholarship, which reads the 2300 evenings-mornings as literal days concerning Antiochus IV in the 2nd century BC. The 1844 terminus is an interpretive conclusion, not a historiographical fact.
GC-18-081.modern_consensus: Critical scholarship dates the 2300 evenings-mornings to the Antiochene crisis (c. 167-164 BC), not to 1844.

sources.title: Correcting Harold Hoehner's Interpretation of Daniel's 70 Weeks (Liberty University)
Daniel 9:24-27: The Sixty-Ninth and Seventieth Weeks - Associates for Biblical Research

GC-18-082.rationale: source_quality: academic source not located within budget for this Millerite sub-point; primary insider histories used. It is well documented that Miller and his associates first expected the terminus in the spring of 1843/1844 and only later (via S. S. Snow's 'seventh-month' message) fixed on the autumn (Oct 22, 1844). The historical fact of the spring-to-autumn shift is accurate; 'the prophecy points to autumn' is the author's interpretive claim.

GC-18-108.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The great Leonid meteor storm occurred on the morning of November 13, 1833, over North America. Firmly established (Olmsted's contemporary study; modern meteor-science histories).

sources.title: The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society
Leonids - Wikipedia

GC-18-109.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Contemporary and modern assessments call the 1833 storm the most extensive on record to that date (Olmsted: 'probably more extensive and magnificent than any similar one hitherto recorded'; estimated tens of thousands to ~100,000+ meteors per hour). The 1911 wording 'most extensive... ever recorded' is accurate for its time.

sources.title: The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society
Leonids - Wikipedia

GC-18-110.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The quotation describes the 1833 display as covering the whole US firmament for hours; consistent with the documented character of the storm and with contemporary accounts of the awe/alarm it produced.

sources.title: The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society

GC-18-111.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Silliman's Journal (the American Journal of Science and Arts) did publish on the 1833 storm; Denison Olmsted's 'Observations on the Meteors of November 13th, 1833' appeared there. The description (seen across North America, from ~2 a.m. to daylight under clear skies) matches the record.

sources.title: The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society

GC-18-112.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The vivid quotation (thousands of meteors radiating from near the zenith to the horizon) is a period description consistent with the documented intensity of the storm (radiant in Leo; extraordinary rates).

sources.title: The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society

GC-18-113.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. The 1833 storm was covered extensively in the contemporary press; a New York Journal of Commerce article of Nov 14, 1833 on the phenomenon is cited in the meteor and Millerite literature. Newspaper coverage of the event is well documented (Library of Congress).

sources.title: How Newspapers Helped Crowdsource a Scientific Discovery: The 1833 Leonid Meteor Storm - Library of Congress
The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society

GC-18-114.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV quotation of Matthew 24:33, correctly attributed to Jesus.

GC-18-115.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Many contemporaries interpreted the 1833 storm in religious/apocalyptic terms (as a herald of judgment); this reaction is well documented in the newspaper record and later cultural memory.

sources.title: How Newspapers Helped Crowdsource a Scientific Discovery: The 1833 Leonid Meteor Storm - Library of Congress

GC-18-116.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. That the storm turned some public attention toward prophecy and boosted the advent message is plausible and asserted in Millerite sources, but the causal magnitude ('many... led to give heed') is a movement claim not independently quantified.

sources.title: The Early Years of Meteor Observations in the USA - American Meteor Society

GC-18-117.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Josiah Litch, an early Millerite minister, published an exposition of Revelation 9 in 1838 (in 'The Probability of the Second Coming of Christ About A.D. 1843') predicting the fall of Ottoman power. The surrounding 1840 events are covered in academic scholarship on the Oriental Crisis.

sources.title: Josiah Litch - Wikipedia
Return of the Ashes: The Concert of Europe and the 1840 Intervention - Oxford Academic

GC-18-118.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Litch's 1838 exposition predicted the overthrow of Ottoman power in August 1840. Well documented.

sources.title: Josiah Litch - Wikipedia
Return of the Ashes: The Concert of Europe and the 1840 Intervention - Oxford Academic

GC-18-119.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. Litch's calculation (150 years + 391 years 15 days) and the quotation are genuinely his, but the precise 'August 11, 1840' pin-point is a contested Millerite proof: critics note Litch's 1838 prediction gave the looser 'August 1840', that the day-precise figure was refined afterward, and that Litch himself later abandoned the fulfillment claim. Genuine historiographical dispute over the exactness.
GC-18-119.modern_consensus: Litch's Aug-11-1840 precision and its 'exact fulfillment' are disputed; Litch dropped the claim in later life.

sources.title: Josiah Litch - Wikipedia
Return of the Ashes: The Concert of Europe and the 1840 Intervention - Oxford Academic

GC-18-120.rationale: source_quality: academic-partial. In the 1840 Oriental Crisis the Ottomans did accept the intervention of the allied European powers (Convention of London, signed July 15, 1840; ultimatum to Muhammad Ali delivered in August), so a real event underlies the claim. But characterizing this as the predicted 'overthrow' of Ottoman power is contested: the Empire did not fall (it endured until 1922), the Convention preceded Aug 11, and the 'at the very time specified' fulfillment is a Millerite interpretation, not a historical consensus.
GC-18-120.modern_consensus: The 1840 events were a Great-Power intervention preserving, not overthrowing, the Ottoman Empire; the 'exact fulfillment' framing is disputed.

sources.title: Convention of London (1840) - Wikipedia
Return of the Ashes: The Concert of Europe and the 1840 Intervention - Oxford Academic

GC-18-121.rationale: source_quality: academic source not located within budget for this sub-point; primary movement history used. From 1840 the movement grew rapidly as educated leaders (Joshua V. Himes, Josiah Litch, Charles Fitch and others) joined in preaching and publishing. Well documented in the Millerite record.

This is consistent with Bliss's portrait of a self-educated, methodical thinker. The 'wisdom of heaven' clause is theological and not testable.

GC-18-142.rationale: source_quality: academic-full. Deanesly's standard monograph documents late-medieval ecclesiastical prohibitions of vernacular Bible reading and 'the withholding of the scriptures from' the laity (esp. after the Waldensian/Lollard movements, e.g. the 1408 Oxford constitutions). The historical core is well supported. Caveat: Deanesly nuances the blanket generalization: there was no single, timeless, universal ban on the Bible itself; restrictions targeted unauthorized vernacular translations and varied by region and period. The present-tense sweeping polemic overstates that documented reality.
GC-18-142.modern_consensus: The medieval church restricted unauthorized vernacular Bibles in specific times/regions rather than imposing a universal ban on Scripture.

sources.title: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible and Other Medieval Biblical Versions (Cambridge UP, 1920)
